content.problemStatement:
Chef is very fond of horses. He enjoys watching them race. As expected, he has a stable full of horses. He, along with his friends, goes to his stable during the weekends to watch a few of these horses race. Chef wants his friends to enjoy the race and so he wants the race to be close. This can happen only if the horses are comparable on their skill i.e. the difference in their skills is less.

There are **N** horses in the stable. The skill of the horse **i** is represented by an integer **S[i]**. The Chef needs to pick 2 horses for the race such that the difference in their skills is minimum. This way, he would be able to host a very interesting race. Your task is to help him do this and report the minimum difference that is possible between 2 horses in the race.

Input:First line of the input file contains a single integer **T**, the number of test cases.

Every test case starts with a line containing the integer **N**.

The next line contains **N** space separated integers where the **i**-th integer is **S[i]**.

Output:For each test case, output a single line containing the minimum difference that is possible.
content.inputFormat: The first line contains an integer T, the number of test cases. Each test case consists of two lines: the first line contains an integer N (the number of horses), the second line contains N space‑separated integers S[i] representing the skill of each horse.
content.outputFormat: For each test case output a single line containing the minimum possible absolute difference between the skills of any two selected horses.

sampleTestCases.explanation: After sorting the skills we get [1,4,9,13,32]. The smallest adjacent difference is 4-1 = 3.
Sorted skills: [10,20,30]. Minimum adjacent difference is 20-10 = 10.
Only one possible pair, difference is |200-100| = 100.

hidden.label: edge case with all equal values

solution.approach:
The minimum difference between any two numbers in a set is always achieved by two consecutive elements after sorting the set. Therefore, for each test case we sort the array of skills in non‑decreasing order and scan once, keeping the smallest difference between adjacent elements.

Steps:
1. Read N and the array S.
2. Sort S.
3. Initialise answer = +infinity.
4. For i from 1 to N‑1 compute diff = S[i] - S[i‑1] and update answer = min(answer, diff).
5. Output answer.

This works because sorting puts numbers that are close together next to each other, guaranteeing that the global minimum will be among those adjacent pairs.
solution.timeComplexity: O(N log N) per test case due to sorting; the linear scan is O(N). Overall O(T * N log N).
solution.spaceComplexity: O(N) to store the skills of a test case (in‑place sorting does not need extra space beyond the array).

code.Python3:
import sys

def solve():
    data = sys.stdin.read().strip().split()
    if not data:
        return
    it = iter(data)
    T = int(next(it))
    out_lines = []
    for _ in range(T):
        N = int(next(it))
        arr = [int(next(it)) for _ in range(N)]
        arr.sort()
        ans = 10**18
        for i in range(1, N):
            diff = arr[i] - arr[i-1]
            if diff < ans:
                ans = diff
        out_lines.append(str(ans))
    sys.stdout.write('\n'.join(out_lines))

if __name__ == "__main__":
    solve()
